exemplar.title: How do the trigonometric functions, calculus derivatives, and vector analysis used in the modelling of car crash scenarios in reconstruction simulations?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do the trigonometric functions, calculus derivatives, and vector analysis used in the modelling of car crash scenarios in reconstruction simulations?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear purpose and focus
Research question is explicitly highlighted and tied throughout the body of the work
Coherent structure with signposted introduction and contents page
weaknesses: Research question phrasing is grammatically flawed and overly broad
Methodology lacks justification for source selection and sample size
In-text citations are incomplete and not consistently linked to the bibliography
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates clear and effective communication of the topic, with an explicit research question and coherent structure that guide the reader. Each mathematical derivation is consistently linked back to the stated purpose, reflecting strong focus. However, minor language lapses in the research question, an overly broad scope covering three mathematical branches, and insufficient methodological justification prevent the award of the maximum band. A mark of 5 accurately reflects high performance with some room for refinement.

strengths: Correct application of kinematic equations, vector components, and impulseâmomentum relations to crash contexts
Effective integration of simulation outputs with theoretical models
Use of technical terminology aligns generally with subject-specific concepts
weaknesses: Occasional conceptual slips (confusing units, misuse of restitution in Eq. 38)
Inconsistent symbol definitions and missing unit discussion in key equations
Recurring spelling and formatting errors in terminology
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student displays good subject knowledge with mostly relevant application of mathematical and physical concepts. Equations are applied appropriately to the crash reconstruction context, and sources are used effectively. Occasional conceptual misunderstandings, inconsistent notation, and typographical errors limit performance to the top of the 3â4 band, warranting a score of 4.

strengths: Good breadth of research using eight cited sources, software tools, and real-world data
Clear logical progression in analysis, linking derivations to simulation results
Reasoned argument structure with insights into simulation limitations
weaknesses: Limited critical appraisal of source reliability and methodological assumptions
Absence of sensitivity analysis or quantitative evaluation of uncertainties
Discussion remains descriptive in places without weighing alternative models or validating results against real data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation shows solid critical thinking: research is generally relevant, analysis is clear, and conclusions are reasoned. The use of multiple data sources and simulations evidences depth. However, the lack of rigorous sensitivity checks, limited critical evaluation of model assumptions, and descriptive rather than quantitative discussion of limitations prevent it from reaching the highest band of critical thinking. A mark of 6 in the 4â6 band rewards the strong analytical work while acknowledging these shortcomings.

strengths: Report follows a clear, numbered structure that supports reader comprehension
Figures and tables are captioned and generally aid understanding
Title, word count, and page numbers are present and correctly placed
weaknesses: Formatting is inconsistent: misaligned tables, broken equation numbering, and erratic spacing
Mixed citation styles and missing source acknowledgements for some images
Some headings and formula blocks are misplaced, disrupting the flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is clear and appropriately structured, facilitating understanding of the investigation. Essential formatting elements (title, word count, page numbers) are correctly applied. Nevertheless, inconsistent layout, citation style variation, and formatting errors (e.g., equation numbering gaps, misaligned tables) limit the work to the mid-band. A score of 3 reflects these strengths and weaknesses fairly.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is plainly stated at the start; however, wording is grammatically flawed (âHow do â¦ usedâ¦â) and the scope is quite broad (three distinct branches of mathematics over multiple crash types).  While subsequent sections return to the question, the breadth means the focus is only **partially** maintained, matching the descriptors of the 3-4 band.  Within that band the clarity earns the higher mark of 4.

The essay contains step-by-step derivations and applies them to three simulation scenarios, but little numeric cross-checking or sensitivity analysis is done.  Calculations are sometimes left symbolic, and conclusions about severity are asserted rather than rigorously shown.  Hence analysis is **clear but only moderately supported**, aligning with the middle of the 4-6 band.

Technical terms such as *tangential acceleration*, *yaw mark*, *impulse* and vector notation are used generally appropriately, but there are repeated spelling/formatting errors ("reconstruction ist", â$a \tan\theta$â mis-typed) and some misuse of symbols.  Hence terminology is **mostly accurate**, meriting a mid-band 3.

Title, word count and page numbers are present, and figures/tables are captioned; however, formatting is inconsistent (mis-aligned tables, broken equation numbering, erratic spacing).  Citations use a mix of styles and some images lack source acknowledgement.  These issues indicate **partial adherence** to layout conventions, fitting 1-2 marks with the higher value of 2.

The student correctly applies kinematic equations, vector components and impulseâmomentum relations to crash contexts and integrates them with simulation outputs, showing **good but not flawless knowledge**.  Occasional conceptual slips (e.g. confusing units, misuse of restitution in Eq. 38) and limited depth in advanced HL mathematics prevent excellence, situating performance at the top of the 3-4 band.

The topic of mathematical tools in crash reconstruction is introduced early, the RQ is explicitly highlighted, and the introduction/contents page sign-post a coherent structure.  Throughout the body the student consistently links each mathematical derivation to the stated purpose of aiding reconstruction, showing **clear purpose and focus**.  Minor language lapses (âreconstruction istâ) cause slight loss of polish, so the uppermost mark in the 5-6 band is not fully warranted, but overall communication remains accurate and effective.

The report follows the expected EE structure with numbered sections, tables and illustrations that generally aid comprehension.  Some headings/figures are misplaced and long formula blocks occasionally disrupt flow, but overall the structure is **clear and supports understanding**, meriting the lower end of the 3-4 band.

Eight cited sources, manufacturer software, and real-world data table demonstrate **good breadth** and are largely relevant to the RQ.  Nonetheless, reliance on generic physics formulae and limited critical appraisal of source reliability stop the work from reaching the excellence of the 7-9 band; a solid 5 within the 4-6 band is appropriate.

A logical progression from evidence to findings is present and a Reflection section acknowledges limitations, yet evaluation remains fairly descriptive (e.g. âsimulation is limitedâ) and does not critically weigh alternative models or quantify uncertainties.  The argument is reasoned but only **partially critical**, warranting 4 in the 4-6 band.

A general methodology is outlined (literature review, Virtual Crash 6 simulations, comparison with published data) and some raw/processed tables are provided.  Nevertheless, source selection is thin (trial version software, small data sample) and many derivations are standard textbook material rather than methodologically gathered evidence.  This reflects a **mostly complete but not comprehensive** approach, placing the work low in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: Footnote formatting is inconsistent with main text citations. Switching from inline HTML sup tags to a uniform referencing style would improve presentation and meet academic standards.
Equation numbering jumps from \(\tag{3}\) to untagged and then \(\tag{4}\). Ensure sequential, consistent tagging of equations to maintain clear cross-referencing.

The research questionâs phrasing is grammatically flawed and overly broad, spanning three mathematical branches and crash types. Consider revising for clarity and narrowing scope to strengthen focus and coherence.
Use of âreconstruction istâ shows a recurring spelling/spacing issue. Ensure consistency in key terminology (e.g., âreconstructionistâ) to maintain professional tone and clarity.
The final sentence of the scope section trails off abruptly and lacks completion. Clarify how trigonometry, calculus, and vectors specifically fit within this scope to guide the reader.

The methodology overview broadly references expert studies and simulation but lacks justification for source selection and sample size. Specify criteria for selecting data and software settings to demonstrate informed methodology.

Section 3.5 discussion reiterates findings descriptively without critically weighing limitations or comparing model predictions to real-world data. Introduce quantitative evaluation to strengthen argument.
In Equation (34) the term \(a_T = r a \times a_t\) appears incorrect; ensure notation distinguishes tangential (\(a_T\)) and normal components clearly, for example \(a_T = r\alpha\).

Vector notation in Equation (15) uses \(v = v_x\vec{a}_x + v_y\vec{a}_y\). Unit vectors should be \(\hat{i},\hat{j}\) or similar; \(\vec{a}_x\) misleads as acceleration vector rather than axis unit vector.
Section 2.3.1 outlines skid-mark analysis but stops short of sensitivity checks for brake efficiency or surface friction. Adding a sensitivity analysis would deepen critical evaluation.
Equation (40) defines restitution coefficient but lacks unit discussion; e is dimensionless but its dependence on speeds requires explicit assumption statement to avoid misunderstanding.
Raw data table displays misaligned cell borders and inconsistent font sizes. Align columns and standardize text styling to improve readability.
Processed data table mixes HTML math tags and plain numbers, breaking formatting consistency. Convert all math expressions into unified LaTeX or text to maintain style.
In-text citations (e.g., Spaulding, n.d.) lack direct link to bibliography. Ensure every citation has a matching completed entry for traceability.
Friction factor calculation yields \(f=1.80\), which is unrealistically high for road surfaces. Re-examine unit conversions and assumptions in \(S_f=\sqrt{30fd}\).
Reflection section remains mainly descriptive of limitations. Deepen reflection by analyzing how setbacks shaped methodology choices and what you would concretely change.
Bibliography entries use varying punctuation and ordering (author, date, publisher). Adopt a single citation style (APA, MLA) consistently for professional presentation.
Simulation setup relies solely on a trial version of Virtual Crash 6 with limited scenarios. Expanding to additional software or real-world test data would strengthen research validity.
Table 3 includes segment drag coefficients unrelated to crash scenarios. Clarify purpose or relocate to appendix to maintain relevance and avoid reader confusion.
Equation (19) describes \(z = gt^2\) but omits units and initial conditions. Adding context on variable definitions (e.g., units, initial velocity) would clarify application.
Symbols \(r\) and \(\theta\) appear without explicit definitions before Equation (30). Define each variable at first use to aid reader comprehension.
Figure 7 caption is placed under three images but only labeled once. Number each view (plan, lateral, front) separately and reference in text for clarity.
The conclusionâs summary restates findings but does not link back to the research questionâs precise wording. Explicitly connect each main point to aspects of the RQ.
